channel.title: The Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Jeff Bechtel.
channel.itunes:author: Jeff Bechtel

item.title: Morning Brief — Wednesday, April 01, 2026
item.description: Your Morning Brief for Wednesday, April 01, 2026. National news, Columbus local, home lending, and AI.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Wednesday, April 1st, 2026. This is The Morning Brief, your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, what's moving in home lending, and the latest in AI. I'm your host. We've got a lot to cover this morning, starting with a national story that is reshaping everything from policy to markets to the streets of American cities. Let's get into it.

A quick look at the markets. Yesterday's session was a big one. The S&P 500 surged nearly 3 percent to close at 6,528. The Dow jumped more than 1,100 points, about 2.5 percent, closing at 46,341. The Nasdaq led the charge with a gain of nearly 4 percent, ending the day at 21,590. It was the best single session for all three major indexes since May of last year. The ten-year Treasury yield eased slightly to 4.30 percent. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at 6.38 percent this week, according to Mortgage News Daily. That is the highest level we have seen in more than six months, and we will dig into what is driving that in the home lending segment.

Let's start with the story that is driving almost everything else right now: the US conflict with Iran, known as Operation Epic Fury, and the extraordinary wave of domestic opposition it has touched off.

Last Saturday, something historic happened on the streets of American cities. An estimated eight to nine million people participated in more than 3,300 organized protests nationwide, under the banner of the No Kings movement. To put that in perspective, organizers and independent analysts are calling it the largest single-day demonstration in American history, surpassing even the Women's March of 2017. The protests stretched from New York City to rural Idaho, and rallies were held in more than a dozen countries across Europe, Latin America, and Australia. Demonstrators turned out to oppose the Trump administration's prosecution of the war with Iran, ICE operations that have resulted in civilian deaths, and what organizers described as a broader threat to democratic institutions. Bruce Springsteen drew an estimated 200,000 people to a rally in Saint Paul with a performance of his recent single, written about federal law enforcement shootings that have galvanized the opposition movement. Protests were largely peaceful, though Los Angeles saw tear gas deployed near a federal detention facility downtown, and a number of arrests were made in Portland.

The war itself may be approaching a critical inflection point. Markets rallied sharply Tuesday on an unconfirmed report that Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian is open to ending hostilities if certain guarantees can be reached. Earlier in the week, President Trump extended a negotiating deadline, a move the bond market watched closely. If a ceasefire or diplomatic agreement materializes, the downstream effects would be enormous: lower oil prices, easing inflation expectations, and a meaningful drop in Treasury yields. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas flows, has been effectively restricted since hostilities began in late February. That energy disruption is the single biggest driver of economic volatility right now, and Tuesday's market surge reflects just how much investors are pricing in a potential resolution.

On Capitol Hill, the House passed a stopgap DHS funding bill by a narrow 213 to 203 vote after Republicans rejected the Senate's version of the deal. The standoff means the Department of Homeland Security remains operating on a patchwork funding arrangement, with the federal immigration enforcement apparatus caught in the middle of a weeks-long congressional stalemate. No resolution appears imminent, and the debate over immigration enforcement funding is increasingly entangled with the broader political fallout from the Iran war protests. The CPAC conference this past weekend offered a window into the Republican base's complicated feelings about the war, with some attendees expressing anxiety about a prolonged conflict even as they backed the administration's broader foreign policy posture.

Shifting gears to home lending, the Iran conflict is doing measurable damage to mortgage affordability, and the numbers are worth understanding clearly. The average thirty-year fixed rate hit 6.38 percent this week, up for the fourth consecutive week. Before Operation Epic Fury launched in late February, that same rate was sitting just under 6 percent. That 40-basis-point jump might sound modest in isolation, but in real terms it adds roughly 63 dollars a month to a typical first-time homebuyer's payment. Over the life of a thirty-year loan, that works out to more than 22,000 dollars in additional interest. For buyers who were already stretching on affordability, that is a meaningful and discouraging shift.

CNN Business is reporting that the housing market is caught in a bind that goes beyond the rate increase itself. Buyer confidence is fragile when oil prices are spiking, gas at the pump is elevated, and the economic outlook carries genuine uncertainty. CNBC notes that the war's effects are already rippling into construction costs, as energy prices push up the price of materials and transportation. That is a double headwind for new housing supply that the market simply does not need right now.

There is a potential tailwind worth flagging. The Federal Open Market Committee held the federal funds rate steady at 3.50 to 3.75 percent at its March meeting. Markets are now beginning to price in a possible cut at the next FOMC meeting, scheduled for April 28th and 29th, particularly if geopolitical risk fades and inflation expectations moderate. A Fed cut does not directly move mortgage rates, but it would send a clear signal that the central bank sees the risk balance shifting toward easing, which typically puts downward pressure on the bond market and, in turn, on fixed rate loan products. For originators and borrowers sitting on the sideline, the next few weeks could represent a meaningful opportunity window if the geopolitical picture improves.

Now to AI, and the story that the technology industry spent most of Tuesday talking about. On March 31st, Anthropic accidentally published internal Claude Code source files to the public npm registry. Specifically, a nearly sixty-megabyte JavaScript source map file, the kind of file normally used only for internal debugging, was inadvertently bundled into version 2.1.88 of the Claude Code package. Security researchers identified it almost immediately. Within hours, the files had been downloaded and mirrored more than 41,500 times on GitHub. Anthropic moved quickly to issue DMCA takedown notices, and GitHub pulled the repositories, but the material was already widely distributed. Bloomberg confirmed the incident Tuesday morning.

What was actually inside? Roughly 512,000 lines of TypeScript, including 44 feature flags representing capabilities that are fully built but not yet shipped to users. The leak also exposed internal model codenames. Capybara refers to a variant of Claude 4.6. Fennec maps to Opus 4.6. And an unreleased model internally called Numbat is apparently still in active testing. Anthropic says no customer data was exposed, and the company attributes the incident to human error in the release pipeline. But the episode raises real questions about supply chain security in AI developer tooling, particularly for enterprises using these platforms in sensitive financial, legal, or operational workflows. The practical risk to most users is low, but the reputational and competitive exposure from a leak of this scale is significant, and the industry will be watching closely how Anthropic manages the aftermath.

On the AI policy front, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed an executive order Monday establishing the state's own framework for AI oversight, directly countering the Trump administration's efforts to restrict state-level AI regulation. The move sets up what could become a significant federal-state collision on AI governance. California is home to Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and most of the major frontier labs, and Newsom's signal is clear: the state intends to set its own standards regardless of what Washington does. Industry observers are watching to see whether other large states align with California's approach or defer to the federal posture.

Meanwhile, OpenAI closed its latest private investment round, drawing capital from Amazon, Nvidia, SoftBank, and Microsoft, among others. Details on the valuation were not immediately disclosed, but the roster of investors signals continued institutional conviction in OpenAI's competitive position, even as the field grows more crowded with serious challengers across multiple fronts. For enterprises evaluating their AI vendor relationships in the wake of this week's Anthropic leak, the investment news is a useful reminder that the frontier lab landscape remains well-capitalized and highly competitive.

Closer to home, let's check in on Columbus. Weather-wise, this is going to be a warm start to April. We are looking at a high around 77 degrees today, with southwesterly winds around 13 miles per hour. The morning and afternoon should be pleasant, but here is the catch: showers and thunderstorms are likely this evening and overnight, with some storms potentially reaching strong-to-severe levels. The Storm Prediction Center has central Ohio in a marginal-to-slight risk category tonight. Get outdoor plans in before dinner, and keep a weather app close as the evening develops.

On the development front, two of the most consequential construction projects in the city's recent history are moving forward in 2026. The Capitol Square Renaissance is the bigger story: a 600-million-dollar redevelopment spanning ten acres in the heart of downtown Columbus. The plans call for more than a thousand new residential units, nine restaurants, nearly 200,000 square feet of office space, and multiple parking decks. If it comes together as designed, it would rank among the most transformative mixed-use projects Columbus has undertaken in a generation. For a downtown that has seen real momentum in recent years, this is the kind of anchor investment that tends to pull additional development behind it.

Just north of downtown, the North Market Merchant Tower is on track to wrap construction this year. The 32-story building will bring a 206-room hotel, more than 170 apartments, 68,000 square feet of office space, and a parking garage to one of the city's most beloved corridors. The North Market has always been an institution, and this tower will bring thousands of new residents and visitors directly into its orbit. NBC4 has been tracking both projects as the central Ohio construction pipeline continues to hum along, even as the national picture gets more complicated.

On the residential side, the Columbus and Central Ohio Regional MLS counted 3,262 closings through the first two months of 2026, essentially flat with the same period a year ago. That stability is worth noting given what is happening nationally. Columbus is outperforming most peer markets, with prices continuing to edge upward despite the rate headwinds from the war. For mortgage professionals in this market, that sustained demand is a foundation to build on when rates eventually ease.

Before I let you go, one thing to watch this week: the Federal Open Market Committee meets April 28th and 29th, and it may be the most consequential Fed meeting in months. After five straight meetings with no change, rate cut speculation is building, particularly if the Iran situation shows signs of genuine resolution. Two data releases between now and then will set the tone. The PCE inflation reading comes out this Friday, and the March jobs report follows next week. If both come in soft, the case for a cut gets substantially stronger, and mortgage rates could see meaningful movement before the end of the month. For anyone in home lending or watching the housing market, those two data points are worth circling on the calendar.

That is your Morning Brief for Wednesday, April 1st. Have a great day. I will see you tomorrow.

item.title: Morning Brief — Tuesday, March 31, 2026
item.description: Your Morning Brief for Tuesday, March 31, 2026. National news, Columbus local, home lending, and AI.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, March 31st, 2026. This is The Morning Brief, your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, what's moving in home lending, and the latest in AI. I'm your host. We're leading with national news today, where the DHS shutdown is now in its sixth week and the ripple effects are hitting airports hard. Let's get into it.

A quick look at the markets from yesterday's session. The S&P 500 closed at around 6,340, down about half a percent. The Dow edged up slightly, gaining about 50 points to close near 45,216. The Nasdaq had a rougher day, falling roughly one percent. The ten-year Treasury yield dropped sharply to 3.92 percent as investors moved into safer assets amid geopolitical jitters and recession concerns. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is averaging 6.38 percent according to Freddie Mac's latest survey, up from 6.22 percent the week before. That marks the highest level in about six months, and we'll have more on what's driving that in the home lending segment.

The Department of Homeland Security shutdown has now stretched past the six-week mark, and the consequences are becoming impossible to ignore. Roughly 61,000 TSA officers have been working without pay since mid-February, and the strain is showing. Last Friday set a record, with more than 3,500 officers calling out sick, about 12 percent of the entire workforce. Wait times at major airports have ballooned past four hours in some cases, and nearly 500 officers have resigned since the shutdown began. That is a significant talent drain for an agency that was already struggling to recruit. Over the weekend, President Trump signed a memorandum directing DHS to use available funds to start paying TSA employees immediately, with paychecks expected to begin going out yesterday. The money comes from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed earlier this year. But here is the catch. The underlying shutdown is not resolved. The House passed a 60-day funding bill, the Senate passed its own version that strips out ICE funding, and both chambers are now in recess until April 13th. So the paychecks may offer some relief, but the political impasse that caused this mess remains firmly in place. If you are flying anytime soon, build in extra time.

Meanwhile, the Treasury Department made a midseason pitch for the administration's tax agenda. Secretary Scott Bessent announced Monday that the Working Families Tax Cuts are, in his words, putting more money back into the pockets of American families, workers, and small business owners. The announcement is timed to the midpoint of the 2026 filing season, when roughly half of all returns are in. The administration is clearly trying to make the tax cuts tangible for ordinary households as filing season makes people unusually aware of what they owe and what they are getting back. Whether voters feel the difference at the kitchen table remains the key political question heading into the fall.

On the economic front, the labor market continues to send mixed signals. The most recent data shows about 130,000 new jobs added, with the unemployment rate holding steady at 4.3 percent. That sounds stable enough on the surface, but zoom out and the picture is more cautious. Annual job growth in 2025 came in at just 181,000 positions for the entire year, the slowest pace outside of a recession on record. The Fed is holding its benchmark rate at 3.5 to 3.75 percent and projecting unemployment to tick up slightly to 4.4 percent by the end of 2026. Inflation is still running around 3 percent, above the Fed's target. Economists are describing this as a low-hire, low-fire environment. Companies are not laying people off in large numbers, but they are not adding headcount aggressively either. It is a labor market that is stable but not exactly inspiring confidence about the road ahead.

And one more national item. The EPA issued a temporary fuel waiver effective May 1st through the 20th that will allow nationwide summer sales of E15 gasoline and suspend federal enforcement of state boutique fuel requirements. The idea is to boost fuel supply and stabilize prices heading into peak summer driving season. If you are planning road trips this summer, this could help keep a few cents off at the pump.

Shifting gears to Columbus and Central Ohio. Weather-wise, Columbus is looking at a high around 73 degrees today with increasing clouds through the afternoon. There is a chance of showers and storms developing later this evening, so keep an umbrella handy if you are heading out after work.

Columbus City Council meets today and will introduce a ceremonial resolution honoring three members of the Ohio Air National Guard who were killed when their KC-135 refueling tanker crashed in Iraq. Master Sergeant Tyler Simmons, 28, and Captain Curtis Angst, 30, were both from Columbus. Captain Seth Koval, 38, was from Stoutsville. The resolution recognizes their service and sacrifice, and it is a somber reminder that Central Ohio's military families continue to bear real costs in conflicts overseas.

On the development and transit front, COTA continues to make moves. The transit authority's board approved nearly 30 million dollars in funding for 37 sidewalk, biking, and trail projects across the region this year. That is on top of the 42 million dollar federal grant COTA secured for the first dedicated transit corridor in Central Ohio. Construction on the Spring Street Terminal renovation continues through May, and a new bus route, Line 30, is now connecting Dublin, Hilliard, Upper Arlington, and Ohio State's campus. For a city that has long been car-dependent, this is a meaningful investment in multimodal infrastructure, and it is worth watching whether ridership numbers follow the money.

And in the housing market, Columbus continues to run hot. The median home price has reached 315,000 dollars, up about 3.3 percent from a year ago. Inventory sits at just 1.6 months, which is firmly in seller's territory. Homes are taking a bit longer to sell, with the median time on market stretching to 49 days from 43 last year, which gives buyers at least a slightly wider window. The demand is being fueled by major employers. Honda, Google, Intel, Amazon, and OhioHealth are all drawing talent to the region, and that population growth is keeping pressure on a housing supply that simply has not kept up.

Now to home lending, where the past few weeks have been a rough ride for anyone watching rates. The thirty-year fixed mortgage rate climbed to 6.38 percent as of last week's Freddie Mac survey, up from 6.22 percent the week before. That represents a jump of roughly 50 basis points since February, when rates briefly dipped below 6 percent for the first time in years. The culprit is a familiar one. Geopolitical tensions, particularly around the situation in Iran, have pushed oil prices higher and reignited inflation fears. Treasury yields spiked, and the mortgage market spread has widened, meaning rates are climbing even faster than underlying bond yields would suggest. Mortgage applications have responded predictably. The MBA reported a 10.5 percent decline in total applications for the week ending March 20th, with refinance applications dropping 15 percent and purchase applications down 5 percent. The spring buying season, which lenders were counting on for a boost, is getting a cold splash of rate reality.

On the regulatory side, a March 13th executive order is drawing significant attention across the industry. President Trump directed the CFPB and FHFA to consider overhauling mortgage regulations for community banks and institutions with under 100 billion dollars in assets. The order calls for tailoring ability-to-repay and qualified mortgage standards for smaller lenders, raising HMDA reporting thresholds to reduce compliance burden, and modernizing appraisal rules to expand the use of desktop appraisals, hybrid appraisals, and AI-powered valuation tools. The order also directed FHFA to lower enterprise housing goals for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac over the next three years, with the single-family low-income goal dropping from 25 to 21 percent and the very low-income goal nearly cut in half, from 6 to 3.5 percent. This signals a clear tilt toward deregulation and credit expansion through smaller banks, though consumer advocates are raising concerns about what reduced housing goals could mean for underserved communities.

And speaking of technology in mortgage, 2026 is shaping up as a pivotal year for AI adoption in the industry. According to recent surveys, 83 percent of lenders plan to increase their generative AI budgets this year, with documented performance improvements including up to 50 percent increases in origination volume and loan closings happening two and a half times faster. The challenge, as always, is infrastructure. Two-thirds of lenders are using or testing AI, but only a fraction have the digital backbone to scale those tools effectively. The regulatory tailwind from that March executive order, which explicitly encourages AI-powered appraisals, could accelerate adoption further.

And finally, the AI and technology beat, where the headlines have been dominated by a collision between Silicon Valley ethics and Pentagon procurement. The Defense Department designated Anthropic, the maker of Claude, as a supply-chain risk after the company refused to allow military use of its technology for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. What happened next was remarkable. More than 30 employees from rival companies OpenAI and Google DeepMind, including Google chief scientist Jeff Dean, filed legal briefs defending Anthropic's position. The timing raised eyebrows because the Pentagon signed a deal with OpenAI almost immediately after blacklisting Anthropic, prompting internal backlash at OpenAI itself. This is a defining moment for the industry. The question of whether an AI company can maintain ethical red lines while competing for government contracts worth billions is no longer theoretical. It is playing out in real time, and the fractures it is creating within these companies are significant.

On the policy front, the White House released its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 20th, outlining seven pillars for federal AI governance. The most notable decision is what it does not do. It explicitly instructs Congress not to create a new federal AI regulator. Instead, the framework calls for sector-specific oversight through existing agencies and industry-led standards. It also pushes for broad federal preemption of state AI laws, which would replace the current patchwork of state regulations with a unified national approach. Industry leaders have largely welcomed this as more workable than navigating 50 different state regimes, though critics argue that without a dedicated regulator, enforcement will lack teeth. The framework also prioritizes child safety measures, intellectual property protection, and maintaining American competitiveness in AI development.

And in the enterprise space, AI adoption continues its rapid climb. A new report from ServiceTitan found that 38 percent of commercial contractors now report measurable business impact from AI tools, more than double the 17 percent figure from 2025. Similar acceleration is happening in HR, where 69 percent of recruiting professionals are now using AI and autonomous agents are being embedded directly into major platforms like ADP. The governance gap, though, is real. Only a small fraction of companies deploying AI in hiring have documented oversight processes, leaving them exposed to compliance risks around algorithmic bias. Companies are moving fast, but the guardrails are not keeping pace.

Before I let you go, one thing to watch this week. Congress is in recess until April 13th, which means the DHS shutdown will almost certainly stretch past the seven-week mark. But keep an eye on whether the TSA payment workaround holds up logistically. If those paychecks actually reach officers this week, it could ease the airport chaos. If they do not, expect the political pressure on both parties to intensify significantly when lawmakers return.

That's your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day. I'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Morning Brief — Monday, March 30, 2026
item.description: Your Morning Brief for Monday, March 30, 2026. National news, Columbus local, home lending, and AI.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Monday, March 30th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief, your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, what's moving in home lending, and the latest in AI. I'm your host. We've got a packed show today, and we're leading with national news, because the Iran conflict just hit a dangerous new chapter, and the markets are feeling every bit of it. Let's get into it.

A quick look at the markets. The S&P 500 closed Friday at 5,368, down 1.67 percent on the session, and now officially in correction territory. The Dow fell 793 points, or 1.73 percent, to 45,166. The Nasdaq dropped 2.15 percent to 20,948. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at 4.44 percent, its highest since July of last year. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is averaging 6.5 percent according to Mortgage News Daily, up more than half a point from where it stood just a month ago. It was the fifth straight losing week for stocks, the longest weekly slide in four years.

So let's talk about what's driving all of this. The US war with Iran reached its 30th day over the weekend, and the situation is escalating on multiple fronts. President Trump told the Financial Times on Saturday that the US is considering seizing Kharg Island, the Persian Gulf facility that handles roughly 90 percent of Iran's oil exports. "Maybe we take Kharg Island, maybe we don't. We have a lot of options," the president said. That kind of rhetoric sent Brent crude surging another 3.5 percent to above 116 dollars a barrel. West Texas Intermediate climbed to 98 dollars. To put that in context, oil was in the low 70s before the first strikes on February 28th. That is a nearly 60 percent spike in one month.

On the diplomatic side, there are some fragile signals of progress. Trump said Iran has agreed to most of a 15-point US demands list, conveyed through Pakistan, and that Tehran has committed to allowing 20 oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz starting today. But the Pentagon is simultaneously preparing for weeks of limited ground operations. The 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit arrived in the region last Friday with transport aircraft and assault vehicles. And the conflict is spreading. Iran-backed Houthis announced over the weekend that they're entering the war, launching ballistic missiles at Israel. The Lebanon front has escalated into full-scale fighting, with more than a thousand militants and civilians killed by late March.

Here's the economic toll. The war has effectively removed about 20 percent of global oil supply from the market, and the ripple effects are everywhere. Moody's AI-driven recession model now puts the probability of a US downturn within twelve months at 49 percent. And here's the number that should get your attention: when backtested over 80 years of data, every single time that model crossed the 50 percent threshold, a recession followed within a year. Energy price spikes have preceded every US recession since World War Two, with the sole exception of COVID. Year to date, the S&P 500 is down roughly 7 percent, the Dow is off about 8, and the Nasdaq has lost more than 10 percent. Futures markets now assign a 31 percent probability of a Fed rate hike in 2026, up from essentially zero just one week ago. That tells you how fast sentiment has shifted.

Meanwhile in Washington, the partial government shutdown is limping toward resolution, at least for TSA workers. The Department of Homeland Security announced that TSA officers would begin seeing paychecks as early as today after working weeks without pay. President Trump used emergency funding authorities from the One Big Beautiful Bill, signed last year, to directly authorize DHS to pay its agents, bypassing a Senate that had failed to reach a deal on appropriations even after holding a vote open for five hours. The move gets airport security lines moving again, but the broader question of DHS funding remains unresolved, and another shutdown could be around the corner.

Also worth noting, the State Department expanded its online presence screening requirements for visa applicants starting today. The new rules now cover K-1 fiance visas, religious worker visas, trainee visas, and humanitarian categories. Expect longer processing times across the board.

Shifting to home lending, and unsurprisingly, the story is all about rates. The thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is now at roughly 6.5 percent, up more than 50 basis points from its late February low of just under 6 percent. That swing happened almost overnight. On February 27th, the day before the Iran strikes began, rates were sitting at 5.99 percent. One month later, they're half a point higher, driven by surging Treasury yields and a flight from risk that has paradoxically pushed borrowing costs up rather than down. The ten-year Treasury yield at 4.44 percent is the highest it's been since last summer, and mortgage-backed securities spreads are widening as uncertainty mounts.

The impact on borrowers is immediate. Mortgage applications fell 10.5 percent as rates climbed, with refinance activity down 15 percent and purchase applications off 5 percent. And the housing market was already showing stress before this rate spike. According to Redfin, there are now 46 percent more home sellers than buyers, a gap of nearly 630,000 properties, the largest in their records going back to 2013. Here's an interesting wrinkle: the median resale home price now exceeds the median price of newly built homes, which is flipping buyer preferences toward new construction.

On the regulatory front, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau continues to face an existential crisis. Headcount is down from 1,700 to roughly 1,300 employees. Acting Director Vought had projected the agency would run out of money in the first quarter, but a federal judge ruled that the CFPB can continue receiving funds from the Federal Reserve, contradicting the administration's position. The tug of war between the White House and the courts over the bureau's future is creating real uncertainty for lenders who need to know what the rules of the road actually are.

The Fed, for its part, held rates steady at 3.5 to 3.75 percent at its March 18th meeting, with the dot plot pointing to one cut later this year and another in 2027. But that decision was made before the worst of the oil shock hit, and the Fed's own statement flagged Middle East oil disruptions as a major threat to its inflation target. Near-term inflation expectations have already risen, and the personal consumption expenditures index is expected to show 2.7 percent inflation on both headline and core measures. The question now is whether the Fed can cut rates at all with oil-driven inflation re-accelerating.

Turning to AI and technology, and the biggest story here is the escalating standoff between Anthropic and the US government. The Department of Defense labeled Anthropic, the company behind Claude, as an "unacceptable risk to national security." The reason? Anthropic has publicly committed to certain red lines, including refusing to enable mass domestic surveillance or fully autonomous weapons systems. The Pentagon's argument is that those commitments could lead the company to disable its technology during wartime, making it unreliable for defense applications. Anthropic has filed suit against the Trump administration, alleging retaliation for its AI safety positions. And in a remarkable show of solidarity, engineers and researchers from Google, OpenAI, and Microsoft have filed court briefs supporting Anthropic's case. That kind of cross-industry alignment on safety principles is unprecedented. Anthropic, meanwhile, is approaching 19 billion dollars in annualized revenue, and the legal battle appears to be helping rather than hurting its talent recruitment.

In other AI news, OpenAI acquired Astral, the startup behind popular Python developer tools including the UV package manager and the Ruff linter. Astral's team will focus on integrating those tools into OpenAI's Codex coding product, which has become a key differentiator in the enterprise market. The company also rolled out GPT-5.2 with a 400,000 token context window and a perfect score on the AIME 2025 math benchmark, plus new mini and nano versions of GPT-5.4 for lighter-weight applications. And in a sign of just how fast product cycles move in this space, OpenAI is shutting down its Sora video generation app, which launched just six months ago in September. The company also released its Responses API, designed to help developers build agentic workflows with shell tool support and hosted container workspaces, a clear signal that the agentic AI paradigm is becoming the central product strategy for frontier labs.

On the policy front, the White House released a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 20th, built around seven pillars including protecting children, respecting intellectual property, and establishing federal preemption over state AI laws. That last piece is significant because frustrated state lawmakers have been passing their own AI regulations while Congress remains deadlocked. The framework is largely deregulatory, focused on limiting developer liability and reducing state-level restrictions to accelerate innovation. Meanwhile, across the Atlantic, the EU Council agreed to streamline its own AI rules, extending timelines for high-risk system compliance by up to 16 months and broadening exemptions for small and mid-cap companies.

Now let's check in on Columbus. Weather-wise, Columbus is looking at highs in the low 60s today with a mix of sun and clouds. A nice change of pace after the severe weather the region has been dealing with.

And speaking of that severe weather, Mayor Ginther reported that the past several weeks have brought unusually intense storms to central Ohio. City crews are still cleaning up downed tree limbs and branches across multiple neighborhoods, and service calls have surged as residents deal with property damage. An EF-1 tornado was confirmed earlier in the week in nearby Morrow County, with peak winds hitting 95 miles per hour. If you've got debris in your yard, the city says crews are working through the backlog, but patience may be required.

On a more forward-looking note, OhioHealth has broken ground on a major new comprehensive cancer center at 3430 OhioHealth Parkway. The five-story facility will span roughly 199,000 square feet and is expected to open in late 2028. It will consolidate cancer services currently spread between Riverside Methodist Hospital and the Bing Cancer Center into a single outpatient hub. For a city that continues to grow its healthcare footprint, this is a significant investment in infrastructure.

And Columbus City Council approved the 2026 operating budget with several amendments to Mayor Ginther's initial proposal. The approved budget adds 7.2 million dollars for the Community Shelter Board, 3 million for the city's 27th pay period, and restores a million dollars to the Elevate Grant program that funds human services nonprofits. The council framed the budget as balancing immediate community needs against long-term fiscal sustainability, which is the kind of language you hear when the numbers are tight. The Shelter Board funding is particularly notable given the ongoing strain on homeless services across central Ohio.

Before I let you go, one thing to watch this week: keep an eye on whether those 20 oil tankers actually transit the Strait of Hormuz as Iran reportedly agreed. If they do, oil prices could ease and markets could catch a relief rally. If they don't, we may be looking at 120-dollar oil and a Fed that is completely boxed in on monetary policy. That single data point could set the tone for April.

That's your Morning Brief for Monday. Have a great start to the week. I'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Morning Brief — Sunday, March 29, 2026
item.description: Your Morning Brief for Sunday, March 29, 2026. National news, Columbus local, home lending, and AI.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Sunday, March the twenty-ninth, twenty twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief, your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, what's moving in home lending, and the latest in AI. I'm your host. Let's get into it.

We start with the national picture, and what a day it was yesterday.

Millions of Americans took to the streets Saturday in the third round of so-called No Kings protests, and by all early accounts, this was the largest yet. Demonstrations were reported in more than three thousand communities coast to coast, from major cities to small towns, all organized around opposition to the Trump administration's domestic and foreign policy agenda. The Iran war, immigration enforcement actions, the deployment of ICE officers to airports, and rising costs of living were all central grievances. The flagship rally in Saint Paul, Minnesota drew a massive crowd, with Bruce Springsteen performing his recent single Streets of Minneapolis, a song about the killings of two people by federal officers, to a deeply energized audience. Significant turnout was also reported in New York, Washington, Los Angeles, Chicago, San Diego, and notably in West Palm Beach, Florida, just miles from the president's residence. Organizers had billed March twenty-eighth as the single largest nonviolent day of action in American history, and while final crowd estimates are still being tallied, early reporting from The Washington Post and NBC News suggests they may not be far off. Police arrested some protesters during a standoff in downtown Los Angeles, but the vast majority of events across the country remained peaceful. To put the scale in perspective, the first No Kings rally in February drew large crowds in about a thousand locations. Saturday's count of over three thousand represents a threefold expansion in just six weeks, suggesting a movement that is growing rather than plateauing.

The protests were fueled in large part by the war in the Middle East, which marked its one-month anniversary this weekend, and the conflict itself took a significant and worrying turn. Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen formally entered the fight on Saturday, launching a salvo of cruise missiles and drones at targets in southern Israel, including the Red Sea resort city of Eilat. Israel's military intercepted most of the incoming fire, but the Houthi involvement opens a dangerous new front and raises the prospect of a wider regional war. This is the same Houthi movement that disrupted global shipping through the Red Sea in twenty twenty-three and twenty twenty-four, so their military capabilities are not theoretical. Meanwhile, at least fifteen U.S. service members were wounded Friday when an Iranian missile struck a Saudi air base hosting American troops, with at least five in serious condition. On the diplomatic front, Vice President Vance told reporters the war will continue, quote, a little while longer, end quote. A coalition of foreign ministers from Pakistan, Turkey, Egypt, and Saudi Arabia met in Islamabad Saturday seeking de-escalation. Iran agreed to allow twenty ships under Pakistani flag to transit the Strait of Hormuz, a small but meaningful gesture amid the tensions. The U.S. had initially projected a four-to-six-week campaign, but reporting from the Times of Israel suggests officials now privately acknowledge it could stretch well beyond that window.

And in a story that drew wide attention for very different reasons, Tiger Woods was arrested Saturday on suspicion of driving under the influence after he struck another vehicle and rolled his Land Rover near his home on Jupiter Island, Florida. The Martin County Sheriff's office confirmed Woods was not injured and that no one in the other vehicle was seriously hurt. It is, of course, not the first time Woods has faced a DUI arrest. He was taken into custody on a similar charge back in twenty seventeen, which he attributed at the time to a reaction to prescription medication. Woods is now fifty years old and had largely stepped back from competitive golf after a serious car accident in twenty twenty-one. The story is developing and no further details on the circumstances have been released.

Closer to home, here is what is happening in Columbus.

Tonight is a big night for Columbus sports fans. The Columbus Aviators make their inaugural debut in the United Football League with a primetime kickoff against the Orlando Storm at eight p.m. Eastern at Inter and Co Stadium in Orlando. The game marks the head coaching debut of Ohio State and NFL legend Ted Ginn Junior, who was hired to lead the expansion franchise through its first season. The UFL is billing this as one of the marquee matchups of opening weekend, and it will air in primetime on a national broadcast. The Aviators' home opener is set for April third at Historic Crew Stadium, where they will host the DC Defenders. That event, dubbed the six-fourteen Kickoff, will feature appearances by Columbus community leaders, the introduction of the team's new mascot, and a halftime performance by Grammy-nominated singer Gavin DeGraw. For a city that has long punched above its weight in professional sports, adding a spring football franchise is another signal of Columbus's growing profile as a major league town.

Central Ohio also joined the national No Kings protests in force on Saturday. According to ABC6, thousands gathered outside the Ohio Statehouse near the William McKinley monument, with more than twenty separate groups organizing events across the metro area. The demonstrations were peaceful and drew a cross-section of the community, from Ohio State students to retirees in the suburbs, many voicing frustration over the Iran conflict, immigration enforcement, and the cost of groceries and housing. Columbus police reported no arrests or significant incidents.

And a nice feather in the cap for the city. A global travel publication recently named Columbus the second-best city in the United States for Gen X to live, citing affordability relative to peer cities, a strong local job market, and the depth of cultural amenities from the Short North arts district to the Metro Parks system. For anyone in real estate or economic development, that kind of national recognition translates directly into relocation interest.

Turning to the mortgage and housing markets, and it has been a rough stretch for anyone hoping spring would bring relief.

Rates continued climbing this past week, extending a streak that has now lasted four consecutive weeks. The thirty-year fixed averaged six point three eight percent as of Thursday, according to Freddie Mac's Primary Mortgage Market Survey, up from six point two two the prior week. That is the largest single-week jump in months. But by Friday, Mortgage News Daily was tracking the thirty-year fixed as high as six point six four percent, which is the highest level in roughly six months. The fifteen-year fixed also jumped to five point seven five percent. The culprit is no mystery. The Iran conflict has rattled global bond markets, pushed oil prices higher, and stoked inflation fears, all of which are putting upward pressure on the ten-year Treasury yield, the benchmark that mortgage rates most closely follow. To put a fine point on it, rates have climbed from below six percent to above six and a half in just about a month. That kind of velocity is painful for borrowers who were on the fence and devastating for anyone who was pre-approved at a lower rate and is now scrambling to requalify.

The impact on demand is starting to show. According to HousingWire, mortgage applications fell ten and a half percent from the prior week, though purchase activity is still running about five percent above year-ago levels. Active inventory ticked up modestly, from roughly seven hundred six thousand to seven hundred fourteen thousand listings nationally, and new listings were slightly positive year over year. So the market is not frozen, but momentum has clearly cooled. The spring buying season that many had hoped would be a turning point for the housing market is instead being complicated by geopolitical uncertainty. For originators and loan officers, the message is clear: lock discipline, transparent communication with borrowers, and rate-buydown strategies matter more than ever in a volatile environment. If you are advising clients right now, the conversation has to be about total monthly payment, not just the headline rate.

On the product innovation front, an interesting development worth flagging. Better Home and Finance and Coinbase have partnered to launch a token-backed mortgage product that allows qualified borrowers to pledge Bitcoin or USDC as collateral to cover conforming down payment requirements without actually liquidating their crypto holdings. It is a niche product today, aimed squarely at crypto-native borrowers who want to maintain their digital asset positions. But it signals continued experimentation at the intersection of digital assets and traditional mortgage finance, and it would not be surprising to see larger lenders explore similar structures if it gains traction.

And in the world of AI and technology, several stories worth your attention.

Anthropic made headlines this week, though not entirely by choice. The company accidentally exposed what appears to be its most capable AI model yet through what sources describe as a basic security lapse. Insiders are calling the leaked model a, quote, step change in reasoning capability, and Anthropic has confirmed the leak occurred, though it has not yet formally announced the model or its specifications. Separately, and perhaps more consequentially for the industry, a federal judge temporarily blocked the Pentagon from labeling Anthropic a supply chain risk. The court suggested the designation may have been retaliatory, tied to Anthropic's refusal to pursue certain military contracts. That case is being closely watched across the tech sector because its outcome could redefine the rules of engagement between AI companies and the Department of Defense. In a notable show of cross-company solidarity, employees from both OpenAI and Google filed briefs supporting Anthropic's legal position.

Google, meanwhile, introduced Gemini three point one Flash Lite, a new efficiency-focused model that delivers two and a half times faster response times and forty-five percent faster output generation compared to earlier Gemini versions, at a price point of just twenty-five cents per million input tokens. For context, that is a fraction of what frontier models cost even a year ago, and it signals that the cost curve for AI inference is continuing to bend downward rapidly. Google also rolled out its Personal Intelligence feature to all U.S. users, allowing Gemini to pull context from Gmail, Photos, YouTube, and other connected apps. Free-tier users now get access, which means millions of people are about to experience genuinely personalized AI for the first time.

And on the regulatory front, two developments to track. The White House released a legislative blueprint for a national AI policy framework urging Congress to adopt a federally unified approach that would preempt the growing patchwork of state AI laws in favor of what the administration calls a light-touch regulatory regime. Think of it as the federal government trying to prevent a situation where AI companies face fifty different compliance regimes. Alongside that, the AI Accountability Act passed this month, requiring companies deploying AI in consequential decisions, and that explicitly includes lending, hiring, healthcare, and criminal justice, to conduct and publish regular bias audits. For anyone in mortgage or financial services, that audit requirement deserves attention now, not when the enforcement deadlines arrive.

That is your Morning Brief for Sunday. Have a great rest of your weekend. I will see you tomorrow.
